lines.text: APPENDIX
Reference notes for the lecturer.
Not intended to be read aloud, but useful for follow-up discussions and student questions.
Key drug summary table:

Drug name | Brand | Target | Payload class | Payload | Linker | DAR | First approval | Major indication | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Gemtuzumab ozogamicin | Mylotarg | CD33 | DNA damage | Calicheamicin | Hydrazone (acid-labile) | ~2-3 | 2000 (withdrawn 2010, returned 2017) | Acute myeloid leukemia | | Brentuximab vedotin | Adcetris | CD30 | Tubulin | MMAE | Valine-citrulline (cleavable) | ~4 | 2011 | Hodgkin lymphoma, ALCL | | Trastuzumab emtansine | Kadcyla (T-DM1) | HER2

Tubulin (maytansine) | DM1 | SMCC (non-cleavable) | ~3.5 | 2013 | HER2+ breast cancer | | Trastuzumab deruxtecan | Enhertu (T-DXd) | HER2 | Topoisomerase I | DXd | GGFG tetrapeptide (cleavable) | ~8 | 2019 | HER2+, HER2-low breast cancer | | Sacituzumab govitecan | Trodelvy | TROP2 | Topoisomerase I | SN-38 | Carbonate (hydrolyzable) | ~7.6 | 2020 | Triple-negative breast, urothelial | | Loncastuximab tesirine | Zynlonta | CD19 | DNA damage | PBD dimer | Valine-alanine (cleavable) | ~2.3 | 2021

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma | | Enfortumab vedotin | Padcev | Nectin-4 | Tubulin | MMAE | Valine-citrulline (cleavable) | ~3.8 | 2019 | Urothelial cancer | | Polatuzumab vedotin | Polivy | CD79b | Tubulin | MMAE | Valine-citrulline (cleavable) | ~3.5 | 2019 | Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
Key acronyms:

ADC: Antibody-drug conjugate DAR: Drug-antibody ratio MMAE: Monomethyl auristatin E MMAF: Monomethyl auristatin F DM1: Maytansinoid 1 DXd: Deruxtecan (exatecan derivative) PBD: Pyrrolobenzodiazepine ILD: Interstitial lung disease ncAA: Non-canonical amino acid T-DM1: Trastuzumab emtansine T-DXd: Trastuzumab deruxtecan TROP2: Trophoblast cell surface antigen 2 HER2: Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2
Discussion questions for class:
Why does T-DXd work in HER2-low breast cancer while T-DM1 does not?
What does this tell you about the importance of payload choice and linker design?
Mylotarg was approved, withdrawn, and reapproved.
What was the key change that allowed it to come back, and what does that history teach us about clinical development?
The bystander effect is both a therapeutic advantage and a source of toxicity.
How would you design an ADC to maximize the advantage while minimizing the toxicity?
Is there a principled trade-off?
Site-specific conjugation produces more uniform ADCs but requires more complex engineering.
When would you choose stochastic conjugation despite the heterogeneity, and when would you invest in site-specific approaches?
ADCs cost hundreds of thousands of dollars per patient.
Is this sustainable as the technology expands to more indications?
What pressures might change the economics over the next decade?
Suggested further reading:
Drago JZ, Modi S, Chandarlapaty S.
"Unlocking the potential of antibody-drug conjugates for cancer therapy.
" Nat Rev Clin Oncol.
Tarantino P, Carmagnani Pestana R, Corti C, et al.
"Antibody-drug conjugates: Smart chemotherapy delivery across tumor histologies.
" CA Cancer J Clin.
The DESTINY-Breast trial series publications, particularly DB03 and DB04, in the New England Journal of Medicine.
The original Mylotarg approval and withdrawal documentation, available through FDA's drugs@FDA database.
Bertozzi CR Nobel lecture on bioorthogonal chemistry, 2022.
